children.title: Nanoscale banded \(90^{\circ}\) domain patterns in (111) oriented \((\mathrm{Pb},\mathrm{La})\mathrm{TiO}_3\) thin films
children.content:
Hong Liu, Zaohui Pu, Dingquan Xiao, and Jianguo Zhu\(^{1}\)  
Department of Materials Science, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China<|txt_split|>Zhihong Wang, Huoding Huang, and Yarong Li  
School of Mathematics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, University of Electronics Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China<|txt_split|>(Received 5 February 2006; accepted 23 July 2006; published online 17 October 2006)<|txt_split|>Lanthanum doped lead titanate ferroelectric thin films were grown on \(\mathrm{Pt / Ti / SiO_2 / Si / (100)}\) substrates using rf magnetron sputtering. X-ray diffraction patterns show that the main phase of the films is tetragonal perovskite with preferred orientation of (111) perpendicular to the substrate. We have also found that the film exhibits a high level of crystallinity and a large amount of crystallites in the films using piezorespect force microscopy. Head-to-tail polarization configurations were often found to exist, indicating that the low electrical energy configurations are quite common in these thin films. Nanoscale banded \(90^{\circ}a\)-d domain patterns as small as \(30 - 60\mathrm{nm}\) in width were observed. The banded domains alternated with adjacent lamellae can be attributed to mechanical vibrations of the lamellae during the transition between the substrate and the film. © 2009 American Institute of Physics, [DOI: 10.1063/1.2357865]

children.title: I. INTRODUCTION
children.content: Great attention has been paid to preparations, characterizations, and applications of ferroelectric thin films in the past decades. Because of their unique pyroelectric and ferroelectric properties, \(\left(\mathrm{Pb}_{0.5}\mathrm{La}_{0.5}\right)\mathrm{Ti}_{1 - x}\mathrm{O}_x\) (PLT) thin films have attracted great interest for most promising materials for room-temperature pyroelectric infrared sensors, infrared thermal imaging devices, etc.<|txt_split|>Determining the domain patterns in ferroelectric films is one of the key requisites for a better understanding of their piezoelectric, pyroelectric, and ferroelectric properties. In addition, the switching of polarization of PLT films is accompanied by a mechanical piezoelectric effect which indicates spatial reorganization of domains. A piezoelectric vibration of the PLT films on a Pt electrode can be induced by applying an electric field of certain frequency \((9\mathrm{kHz})\) to the electrode. The measurement of the vibration amplitude on the film surface allows one to assign the polarization state and direction of ferroelectric films by piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM).<|txt_split|>Studies on the local ferroelectric properties of a film at the nanoscale will provide an effective way not only for achieving insights into fundamental issues such as polarization reversal, domain switching, and domain structure but also for optimizing the properties of the film. In this paper, the thin films were grown on \(\mathrm{Pt / SiO_2 / Si / SiO}\) 100 substrates using rf magnetron sputtering. The crystalline and domain properties were studied by x-ray diffraction (XRD) and PFM.

children.title: II. EXPERIMENT
children.content: A conventional process for mixing oxide was used to prepare the lanthanum doped lead titanate ceramic targets for sputtering. Ceramics with the nominal composition \((\mathrm{PbO}_{0.5}\mathrm{La_{0.5}Ti_{0.5}O_3})\) (PLT) were synthesized, and excessive \(10\mathrm{mol}\%\) PbO was added to compensate the volatilization of the catalysts. The samples were then mixed with other materials used in this study were \(\mathrm{La}_2\mathrm{O}_3\) \(\mathrm{TiO_2}\) and \(\mathrm{PbO}\) with purities of \(99.9\%\) \(99.0\%\) ,and \(99.0\%\) respectively. They were milled in a polyethylene jar with agate balls for \(24\mathrm{h}\) using ethanol as a medium, dried and calcined at \(850^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(2\mathrm{h}\) .The calcined powders were milled into fine powder again, and then poly(vinylalcohol) was added as a binder. The powders with binder were subsequently granulated and mixed with water for \(10\mathrm{min}\) and \(10\mathrm{mm}\) thickness under \(40 - 60\mathrm{MPa}\) uniaxial pressure and sintered at \(1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(2\mathrm{h}\) to form the PLT ceramic targets.<|txt_split|>The PLT ferroelectric thin films with thickness of \(1\mu \mathrm{m}\) were fabricated on \(\mathrm{Pt / Ti / SiO_2 / Si(100)}\) substrates by using rf magnetron sputtering system. For the sake of good compatibility with planar technology of semiconductors and avoid the formation of defects, the film was coated with a layer of \(\mathrm{TiO}_2\) that could not be too high during deposition. Hence, we first deposited films at substrate temperature of \(300^{\circ}C\) and at a pressure of \(2\mathrm{Pa}\) by using pure argon and annealed them at various temperatures ex situ by using conventional thermal annealing (CTA) processing in a lead compensated atmosphere to achieve desired crystallinity. The heating rate from \(150^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\) to \(400^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\) was a desired temperature in \(3^{\circ}\mathrm{C / min}\) and the sintering time is \(1\mathrm{h}\). Typical deposition conditions are shown in Table I.<|txt_split|>The crystallographic analysis of the PLT thin films has been carried out by x-ray diffraction (DX-1000). The topography and domain patterns of the PLT thin films were obtained by means of a scanning force microscope (SPM)<|txt_split|>(Sekio SPA 300) in piezoreson mode, i.e., PFM, in which an ac voltage \( V_{\mathrm{ac}} = V_{\mathrm{S}} \sin (5 \mathrm{~V}, 9 \mathrm{kHz}) \) is applied between the tip and the bottom electrode (Pd) of the sample. The nanoscale displacements induced by the electric fields were measured using a high-speed scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The surface of PLT films due to inverse piezoelectric effect when an alternating electric field was applied between the tip and the surface of PLT films. All scans were performed at room temperature using a conductive Au coated Si,N2cap-tilever (Seiko) with an elastic constant of \( 1.4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m} \) and an integrated tip of about \( 20 \mathrm{~nm} \) in diameter. Furthermore, sintering at \( 650^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \) for \( 1 \mathrm{~h} \) acquisition of signals derived from wavy surface of film allows us to compare topographic and piezoelectric information.

children.title: TABLE I. Typical deposition conditions of the PLT thin films.
children.content:
ItemParameter
Target\( {\mathrm{{Ph}}}_{3}{\mathrm{{La}}}_{2}{\mathrm{{TiO}}}_{3} \) ceramics
Substrate\( \mathrm{P}/\mathrm{{Ti}}/{\mathrm{{SiO}}}_{2}/\mathrm{{Si}}/{100} \)
Target-substrate distance40 mm
Substrate temperature\( {300}^{ \circ }\mathrm{C} \)
Working gas composition\( {9.1}\left( {\mathrm{\;A}{\mathrm{t}}_{2}{\mathrm{O}}_{5}}\right) \)
Working pressure2 Pa
rf input power density\( {2.5}\mathrm{\;W}/{\mathrm{{cm}}}^{2} \)
Deposition time\( 3\mathrm{\;h} \)
Deposition rate0.9 A/s

children.title: A. X-ray diffraction
children.content: To optimize the preparation conditions, PLT thin films were firstly deposited on \(\mathrm{SiO}_2 / \mathrm{Si}(100)\) substrates at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) and then annealed via CTA at a heating rate of \(3^{\circ}\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min}\) from room temperature to \(575^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\). The following figure 1 shows the XRD patterns of PLT thin films on \(\mathrm{SiO}_2 / \mathrm{Si}(100)\) substrates as deposited and annealed at 550, 575, 600, and \(625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\), respectively. It can be seen clearly from Fig. 1 that the PLT thin film sputtered at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) is amorphous. After annealing at 550 and \(575^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\), the PLT film was completely evaporated from the substrate film, although the intensity of the pyrochlore phase (444) in the PLT film annealed at \(575^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\) is remarkably weak.<|txt_split|>The pure perovskite phase could be obtained and the pyrochlore phase completely disappeared when the PLT thin films annealed at \(600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\). However, the pyrochlore phase appears again in films annealed at \(625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) due to the volatilization loss of lead. The PLT thin films deposited on \(\mathrm{Pt / Si / SiO_2 / Si(100)}\) substrates sputtered at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) were also amorphous, hence they need to be annealed at certain temperatures to achieve a high crystalline. According to the analysis in Fig. 1, the optimal annealing condition of PLT thin films sputtered at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) on \(\mathrm{Pt / Si / SiO_2 / Si(100)}\) substrate should be \(600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\).<|txt_split|>Figure 2 shows the XRD pattern of the PLT thin film on \(\mathrm{Pt / Ti / SiO_2 / Si(100)}\) substrate annealed at \(600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\). Figure 2(b) shows the XRD patterns with \(\Delta \theta\) ranging from \(38^{\circ}\) to \(42^{\circ}\), which allows one to clearly separate the diffraction peaks from the two peaks. The peak at \(38^{\circ}\) is located at that of the Pt (111). There are only four diffraction peaks: (111) and (222) of PLT film, (111) of Pt film, and an undetermined peak, which may be a nonperovskite phase, respectively. Figure 2(b) clearly shows that perovskite structure with (111) orientation forms in the PLT films. The preferred orientation may be primarily attributable to the Pt electrode surface. In addition, the Pd surface is also observed in (111) preferred orientation of tetragonal perovskite phase in PLT film depends on the deposition and annealing condi<|txt_contd|>tions. Furthermore, a very small amount of nonpovskite crystals coexist with the dominant povskite phase in the PLT film.

children.title: FIG. 1. XRD patterns of PLT01 films on \(\mathrm{SiO}_2 / \mathrm{Si}(00)\) substrates: (a) as a function of the concentration of \(\mathrm{SiO}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Si} / \mathrm{Si}\) ratio, (b) as a function of the concentration of \(\mathrm{SiO}_2\) and \(625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), respectively, for 1 h, \(\mathrm{Fe_3}\) perovskite plane and \(\mathrm{P}_2\mathrm{S}\) chrysophore plane.

FIG. 2. (a) XRD patterns of the PLT film on \(\mathrm{Pt / Ti / SiO_2 / Si(100)}\) substrate annealed at \(600^{\circ}C\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\). (b) XRD patterns with \(2\theta\) ranging from \(38^{\circ}\) to \(42^{\circ}\) which allows one to clearly separate the diffraction peak of perovskite phase (111) of PLT film from that of Pt (111). Perovskite phase:
B. The topography and domain patterns
children.content: Figures 3(a)-(3c) show the simultaneously acquired topography image and the corresponding piezoresonite images of in-plane polarization (IPP) and out-of-plane polarization (OPP) of \(2 \times 2 \mu \mathrm{m}^2\) area of the PLT film, respectively.<|txt_split|>The topography image (atomic force microscopy image in contact mode) in Fig. 3(a) shows that the root mean square (rms) roughness of the film is about \(4\mathrm{nm}\), indicating that the film has a good surface roughness. The preparation conditions have rather smooth and dense surface.<|txt_split|>As shown in Figs. 3(b) and 3(c), there is scarcely any OPP in the PLT film as indicated by the approximate zero piezoresponse signal in Fig. 3(c), but banded domain patterns can be clearly seen in Fig. 3(b). Figure 3(d) illustrates the suggested domain patterns for the observed IPP around line A-A' in Fig. 3(b). Most of these banded domains can be identified as a band of two main patterns with the polarization vector lying in the \(x-y\) plane from the mapped IPP and geometrical considerations.8,9<|txt_split|>Figure 3(b) also clearly shows that parallel stripes alternated between brightness and darkness appears in most of the grains. The bright or lamellae correspond to the \(a_1\) or \(a_2\) domain, as schematically drawn in Fig. 4, respectively.<|txt_split|>The thickness of lamellae domains can be easily measured by means of line scan piezoresonse point aligned lines \(A - A^{\prime}\) and \(B - B^{\prime}\) in Fig. 3(b) crossing the parallel stripes, corresponding to the same direction of the laser beam (Fig. 3(c), 3(d), 3(e), 3(f), respectively. It is indicated from Figs. 3(e) and 3(f) that<|txt_contd|>\(a - a\) domain slices were about \(1\mu \mathrm{m}\) in height and 30 and \(60~\mathrm{nm}\) in width. The thicknesses of the domain slices are not the same, because they can be correlated with stress conditions.[30]

children.title: FIG. 3. (Color online) The PFM images of PLT thin film: (a) the topography of the film, (b) the topographic area of the film, and (c) the topographic area of the film (IPV), (d) the corresponding piezoresonance image of out-of-plane polarization (OPP); (e) suggested domain pattern for the observed IPP round plane in the sample, (f) the topographic area of the film, and (g) the topographic area of \(A - A^{\prime}\) (a b), and the line scan piezoresonance signal along \(A - B^{\prime}\) in (b).
FIG. 4. Schematic drawing of \(90^{\circ}\) a-domain structure of PLT thin film.
C. Stress and strain
children.content: The domain patterns in a ferroelectric film occur as the result of phase transition from the paraelectric to ferroelectric state to minimize the elastic energies of the system at the expense of creating domain boundaries and developing nonuniform elastic fields near the film/substrate interface. It is important to note that the domains are not necessarily closed, but rather refers to relieve stresses associated with constrained transformation strains and previously unrelied strains in the high temperature phase.3 That is to say that the formation of alternating banded domain with adjacent lamellae is induced by the mechanical strain at the surface and at the interface between the film and the substrate since such a domain structure can minimize the mechanical energy of the film and therefore be more stable.4 It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the intrinsic stress stresses are much lower than those of \(100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) domains.6-8 The inner stresses in films can be divided in two classes: the intrinsic inner stress and the thermal inner stress. The intrinsic inner stress results from the compressive stresses caused by continuous injection of ions with relatively high energy into the film surface and the tensile stresses caused by micropores in film trying to shrink their surfaces. The thermal inner stress mainly comes from different types of stress, such as microtensile stress between the film and the substrate. It can also be introduced in the processing of CTA due to nonuniform heat transmission.<|txt_split|>It is noted that Fig. 3(b) can be separated into two regions (region I and region II), white curved line. Compared with region II which is a very small part of the whole area, region I is full of parallel slices. It can be seen clearly that the white curved line goes along grain boundaries which is also the boundaries of stresses. Based on the experiments in Ref. 11, we conclude that the different domain patterns in Fig. 3(b) are similar to those observed in the same grain region I and region II. In addition, Figs. 3(a) and 3(c) indicate that the domain slices around site \(B - R^{\prime}\) in Fig. 3(b) are thinner and denser than those around site \(A - A^{\prime}\) due to the different stress fields. The nonuniform stress field developed inside each grain involves local deformations. The domain<|txt_contd|>reorganization favors the local deformation due to the high rigidity of PLT films. These rearrangements are more efficient than those in the case of a single-layer film. The size is reduced. In our experimental results, the width of lamellae domain of PLT thin film varies between 30 and \(60~\mathrm{nm}\). The fragmentation of ferroelectric domains allows the formation of a new mechanical energy of the material during domain forming.[21]<|txt_split|>Normally, the polarization vectors adopt a head-to-tail arrangement across the boundary to minimize the amount of the space charges,[2] as shown in Fig. 3(d). Most researchers believe that \(90^{\circ}\) domain arrangements follow head-to-tail arrangements.[1] However, it is not clear how much the \(90^{\circ}\) domains may not predominate if no long-range elastic energy is taken into account, since the head-to-tail alignment of dipoles along the \(90^{\circ}\) domain wall can release the as-induced elastic energy more effectively than the \(180^{\circ}\) domains. In fact, the recent ab initio calculations claimed that the energy of \(90^{\circ}\) domain wall was much lower than that of \(180^{\circ}\) domain wall in bulk \(\mathrm{PbTiO_3}\).[4] According to the scheme proposed by [5], the energy of \(90^{\circ}\) domain wall can be found that head-to-tail polarization configurations often exist in PLT thin films, indicating that the low electrical energy configurations are quite common in PLT thin films.

children.title: IV. CONCLUSIONS
children.content: PLT thin films were prepared on \(\mathrm{Pt / Ti / SiO_2 / Si / 100}\) substrates by using rf magnetron sputtering. The optimal preparation conditions were to deposit at \(300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) and sequentially to anneal at \(600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) for \(1\mathrm{h}\). XRD characterization was performed on a Perkin Elmer diffractometer equipped with a Perovskite phase. PFM imaging shows the existence of nanoscale banded \(\alpha -a\) \(90^{\circ}\) ferroelectric domain patterns with<|txt_contd|>lamellae of \(30 - 60\mathrm{nm}\) in thickness in these films. Alterating banded domain with adjacent lamellae were observed and attributed to the mechanical strains at the surface and at the interface between film and substrate. Head-to-tail polarization configurations were also often observed, indicating that the presence of an additional energy configurations are quite common in PLT thin films.
